title: Royal Society of Edinburgh

[Science](/wiki/Science "Science") and [technology](/wiki/Technology "Technology") [Arts](/wiki/The_arts "The arts") [Humanities](/wiki/Humanities "Humanities") [Social science](/wiki/Social_science "Social science") [Business](/wiki/Business "Business") [Public service](/wiki/Public_service "Public service")
RSE Scotland Foundation RSE Young Academy of Scotland

(**RSE**) is Scotland's [national academy](/wiki/National_academy "National academy") of science and letters. It is a registered charity that operates on a wholly independent and non-partisan basis and provides public benefit throughout [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"). It was established in 1783. As of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Royal_Society_of_Edinburgh&action=edit), there are around 1,800 [Fellows](/wiki/Fellowship_of_the_Royal_Society_of_Edinburgh "Fellowship of the Royal Society of Edinburgh").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Scottish academy of sciences

Royal Society of Edinburgh

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Trans_Royal_Society_of_Edinburgh_cover.jpg/250px-Trans_Royal_Society_of_Edinburgh_cover.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trans_Royal_Society_of_Edinburgh_cover.jpg)

The cover of a 1788 volume of the journal *Transactions of the Royal Society of Edinburgh*. This is the issue where [James Hutton](/wiki/James_Hutton "James Hutton") published his *[Theory of the Earth](/wiki/Theory_of_the_Earth "Theory of the Earth")*.

The **Royal Society of Edinburgh** (**RSE**) is Scotland's [national academy](/wiki/National_academy "National academy") of science and letters. It is a registered charity that operates on a wholly independent and non-partisan basis and provides public benefit throughout [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"). It was established in 1783. As of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Royal_Society_of_Edinburgh&action=edit), there are around 1,800 [Fellows](/wiki/Fellowship_of_the_Royal_Society_of_Edinburgh "Fellowship of the Royal Society of Edinburgh").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

The Society covers a broader range of fields than the [Royal Society](/wiki/Royal_Society "Royal Society") of London, including [literature](/wiki/Literature "Literature") and [history](/wiki/History "History").[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) The Fellowship includes people from a wide range of disciplines: science and technology, arts, humanities, medicine, [social science](/wiki/Social_science "Social science"), business, and [public service](/wiki/Public_service "Public service").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Society_of_Edinburgh&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/20160816_Royal_So._Edin._front_hall.jpg/250px-20160816_Royal_So._Edin._front_hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:20160816_Royal_So._Edin._front_hall.jpg)

Front Hall of Royal Society of Edinburgh building

At the start of the 18th century, [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh")'s intellectual climate fostered many clubs and societies (see [Scottish Enlightenment](/wiki/Scottish_Enlightenment "Scottish Enlightenment")). Though there were several that treated the arts, sciences and medicine, the most prestigious was the Society for the Improvement of Medical Knowledge, commonly referred to as the Medical Society of Edinburgh, co-founded by the mathematician [Colin Maclaurin](/wiki/Colin_Maclaurin "Colin Maclaurin") in 1731.

Maclaurin was unhappy with the specialist nature of the Medical Society,[[4]](#cite_note-4) and in 1737 a new, broader society, the Edinburgh Society for Improving Arts and Sciences and particularly Natural Knowledge, was split from the specialist medical organisation, which then went on to become the [Royal Medical Society](/wiki/Royal_Medical_Society "Royal Medical Society").

The cumbersome name was changed the following year to the Edinburgh Philosophical Society. With the help of [University of Edinburgh](/wiki/University_of_Edinburgh "University of Edinburgh") professors like [Joseph Black](/wiki/Joseph_Black "Joseph Black"), [William Cullen](/wiki/William_Cullen "William Cullen") and [John Walker](/wiki/John_Walker_(naturalist) "John Walker (naturalist)"), this society transformed itself into the Royal Society of Edinburgh in 1783, and in 1788 it issued the first volume of its new journal *Transactions of the Royal Society of Edinburgh*.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Towards the end of the century, the younger members such as [James Hall](/wiki/James_Hall,_4th_Baronet "James Hall, 4th Baronet") embraced [Lavoisier](/wiki/Lavoisier "Lavoisier")'s new nomenclature[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] and the members split over the practical and theoretical objectives of the society. This resulted in the founding of the [Wernerian Society](/wiki/Wernerian_Society "Wernerian Society") (1808–58), a parallel organisation that focused more upon natural history and scientific research that could be used to improve Scotland's weak agricultural and industrial base. Under the leadership of Prof. [Robert Jameson](/wiki/Robert_Jameson "Robert Jameson"), the Wernerians first founded *Memoirs of the Wernerian Natural History Society* (1808–21) and then the *[Edinburgh Philosophical Journal](/wiki/Edinburgh_Philosophical_Journal "Edinburgh Philosophical Journal")* (1822, *Edinburgh New Philosophical Journal* from late 1826), thereby diverting the output of the Royal Society's *Transactions*. Thus, for the first four decades of the 19th century, the RSE's members published articles in two different journals. By the 1850s, the society once again unified its membership under one journal.

During the 19th century, the society contained many scientists whose ideas laid the foundation of the modern sciences. From the 20th century onward, the society functioned not only as a focal point for Scotland's eminent scientists but also for the arts and humanities. It still exists today and continues to promote original research in Scotland.

In February 2014, Dame [Jocelyn Bell Burnell](/wiki/Jocelyn_Bell_Burnell "Jocelyn Bell Burnell") was announced as the society's first female president, taking up her position in October.[[6]](#cite_note-Scotsman_5Feb2014-6)

### The Young Academy of Scotland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Society_of_Edinburgh&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: The Young Academy of Scotland")]

The [Young Academy of Scotland](/wiki/Young_Academy_of_Scotland "Young Academy of Scotland") was founded by the RSE in 2011. It aims to bring together young professionals (aged mid-20s to 40s) from the widest range of disciplines and regions in Scotland to provide ideas and direction for challenges facing Scotland. The members are roughly equal numbers of women and men, serve for five years and are selected from applicants every two years. In 2021 there were 134 members.[[7]](#cite_note-YA-7)

### Location

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Society_of_Edinburgh&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Location")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Royal_Society_Edinburgh.jpg/250px-Royal_Society_Edinburgh.jpg)](/wiki/File:Royal_Society_Edinburgh.jpg)

The Royal Society building, at the junction of George Street and Hanover Street in the [New Town, Edinburgh](/wiki/New_Town,_Edinburgh "New Town, Edinburgh")

The Royal Society has been housed in a succession of locations:[[8]](#cite_note-Waterston-8)

* 1783–1807: College Library, [University of Edinburgh](/wiki/University_of_Edinburgh "University of Edinburgh")
* 1807–1810: Physicians' Hall, [George Street](/wiki/George_Street,_Edinburgh "George Street, Edinburgh"); the home of the [Royal College of Physicians of Edinburgh](/wiki/Royal_College_of_Physicians_of_Edinburgh "Royal College of Physicians of Edinburgh")
* 1810–1826: 40–42 George Street; shared with the [Society of Antiquaries of Scotland](/wiki/Society_of_Antiquaries_of_Scotland "Society of Antiquaries of Scotland") from 1813
* 1826–1908: the Royal Institution (now called the [Royal Scottish Academy Building](/wiki/Royal_Scottish_Academy_Building "Royal Scottish Academy Building")) on [the Mound](/wiki/The_Mound "The Mound"); shared, at first, with the [Board of Manufactures](/wiki/Board_of_Manufactures "Board of Manufactures") (the owners), the Institution for the Encouragement of the Fine Arts in Scotland and the Society of Antiquaries of Scotland
* 1908–1909: University premises at High School Yards
* 1909–present: 22–24 George Street, purchased from the Edinburgh Life Assurance Company with the assistance of a grant of £25,000 from the [Scottish Office](/wiki/Scottish_Office "Scottish Office")

## Awards and medals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Society_of_Edinburgh&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Awards and medals")]

### Fellowship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Society_of_Edinburgh&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Fellowship")]

Main pages: [Fellow of the Royal Society of Edinburgh](/wiki/Fellow_of_the_Royal_Society_of_Edinburgh "Fellow of the Royal Society of Edinburgh") and [Category:Fellows of the Royal Society of Edinburgh](/wiki/Category:Fellows_of_the_Royal_Society_of_Edinburgh "Category:Fellows of the Royal Society of Edinburgh")

[Fellowship of the Royal Society of Edinburgh](/wiki/Fellow_of_the_Royal_Society_of_Edinburgh "Fellow of the Royal Society of Edinburgh") is an award in its own right[[9]](#cite_note-9) that entitles [fellows](/wiki/Fellow "Fellow") to use of the [initialism](/wiki/Initialism "Initialism") or [post-nominal letters](/wiki/Post-nominal_letters "Post-nominal letters") FRSE in official titles.

### Royal Medals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Society_of_Edinburgh&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Royal Medals")]

The Royal Medals are awarded annually, preferably to people with a Scottish connection, who have achieved distinction and international repute in either life sciences, physical and engineering sciences, arts, humanities and social sciences or business and commerce. The Medals were instituted in 2000 by Queen Elizabeth II, whose permission was required to make a presentation.[[10]](#cite_note-medallists-10)

Past winners include:[[11]](#cite_note-11)

* 2020: Peter Kennedy
* 2019: [Nicola Benedetti](/wiki/Nicola_Benedetti "Nicola Benedetti")
* 2018: David Climie, [Richard Henderson](/wiki/Richard_Henderson_(biologist) "Richard Henderson (biologist)") and [Thea Musgrave](/wiki/Thea_Musgrave "Thea Musgrave")
* 2017: [Tessa Holyoake](/wiki/Tessa_Holyoake "Tessa Holyoake")[[12]](#cite_note-12)
* 2016: [James Hough](/wiki/James_Hough "James Hough") and [Angus Stewart Deaton](/wiki/Angus_Deaton "Angus Deaton")
* 2015: *No award*
* 2014: [W. B. Kibble](/wiki/Tom_W._B._Kibble "Tom W. B. Kibble") and [Richard G. Morris](/wiki/Richard_G._Morris "Richard G. Morris")[[13]](#cite_note-13)
* 2023: [Muhammad Ali Imran](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali_Imran "Muhammad Ali Imran") (elected Fellow, FRSE)
* 2013: [John Cadogan](/wiki/John_Cadogan "John Cadogan"), Michael Ferguson and [Ian Wood](/wiki/Ian_Wood_(businessman) "Ian Wood (businessman)")[[14]](#cite_note-14)
* 2012: David Milne and [Edwin Southern](/wiki/Edwin_Southern "Edwin Southern")[[15]](#cite_note-15)
* 2011: [Baroness Helena Kennedy](/wiki/Helena_Kennedy,_Baroness_Kennedy_of_The_Shaws "Helena Kennedy, Baroness Kennedy of The Shaws"), [Noreen Murray](/wiki/Noreen_Murray "Noreen Murray") and Desmond Smith[[16]](#cite_note-16)
* 2010: [Fraser Stoddart](/wiki/Fraser_Stoddart "Fraser Stoddart") and James MacMillan
* 2009: [James Mirrlees](/wiki/James_Mirrlees "James Mirrlees"), [Wilson Sibbett](/wiki/Wilson_Sibbett "Wilson Sibbett"), and [Karen Vousden](/wiki/Karen_Vousden "Karen Vousden")
* 2008: [Roger Fletcher](/wiki/Roger_Fletcher_(mathematician) "Roger Fletcher (mathematician)"), [Richard Holloway](/wiki/Richard_Holloway "Richard Holloway"), and [David Lane](/wiki/David_Lane_(oncologist) "David Lane (oncologist)")
* 2007: David Carter, John David M H Laver, and [Thomas F. W. McKillop](/wiki/Tom_McKillop "Tom McKillop")
* 2006: [John M. Ball](/wiki/John_M._Ball "John M. Ball") and [David Jack](/wiki/David_Jack_(scientist) "David Jack (scientist)")
* 2005: [David Edward](/wiki/David_Edward "David Edward") and [William G. Hill](/wiki/William_G._Hill "William G. Hill")
* 2004: [Philip Cohen](/wiki/Philip_Cohen_(British_biochemist) "Philip Cohen (British biochemist)"), [Neil MacCormick](/wiki/Neil_MacCormick "Neil MacCormick"), and [Robin Milner](/wiki/Robin_Milner "Robin Milner")
* 2003: [Paul Nurse](/wiki/Paul_Nurse "Paul Nurse"), [James Mackay](/wiki/James_Mackay,_Baron_Mackay_of_Clashfern "James Mackay, Baron Mackay of Clashfern") and [Michael Atiyah](/wiki/Michael_Atiyah "Michael Atiyah")
* 2002: [Alfred Cuschieri](/wiki/Alfred_Cuschieri "Alfred Cuschieri"), [Alan Peacock](/wiki/Alan_T._Peacock "Alan T. Peacock"), and [John R Mallard](/wiki/John_Mallard "John Mallard")
* 2001: [James Black](/wiki/James_Black_(pharmacologist) "James Black (pharmacologist)"), [Tom Devine](/wiki/Tom_Devine "Tom Devine"), and A Ian Scott
* 2000: [Kenneth Murray](/wiki/Kenneth_Murray_(biologist) "Kenneth Murray (biologist)"), [Peter Higgs](/wiki/Peter_Higgs "Peter Higgs"), and [Walter Perry](/wiki/Walter_Perry "Walter Perry")

### Lord Kelvin Medal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Society_of_Edinburgh&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Lord Kelvin Medal")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Royal_Society_of_Edinburgh "Special:EditPage/Royal Society of Edinburgh") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(February 2023)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

The Lord Kelvin Medal is the Senior Prize for physical, engineering, and informatics sciences. It is awarded annually to a person who has achieved distinction nationally and internationally, and who has contributed to wider society by the accessible dissemination of research and scholarship. Winners receive a silver medal and are required to deliver a public lecture in Scotland. The award is named after [William Thomson, 1st Baron Kelvin](/wiki/William_Thomson,_1st_Baron_Kelvin "William Thomson, 1st Baron Kelvin") (1824–1907), who was a famous mathematical physicist and engineer, and professor of natural philosophy at the [University of Glasgow](/wiki/University_of_Glasgow "University of Glasgow"). Senior Prize-winners are required to have a Scottish connection but can be based anywhere in the world.

### Keith Medal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Society_of_Edinburgh&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Keith Medal")]

Main article: [Keith Medal](/wiki/Keith_Medal "Keith Medal")

The Keith Medal was historically awarded every four years for a scientific paper published in the society's scientific journals, preference being given to a paper containing a discovery. It was awarded alternately for papers on mathematics and those on earth and environmental sciences. The medal was founded in 1827 as a result of a bequest by [Alexander Keith of Dunnottar](/wiki/Alexander_Keith_of_Dunnottar "Alexander Keith of Dunnottar"), the first treasurer of the Society.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### Lady Margaret Moir Medal (formerly the Makdougall Brisbane Prize)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Society_of_Edinburgh&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Lady Margaret Moir Medal (formerly the Makdougall Brisbane Prize)")]

Further information: [List of Makdougall Brisbane Prize winners](/wiki/List_of_Makdougall_Brisbane_Prize_winners "List of Makdougall Brisbane Prize winners")

The Lady Margaret Moir Medal recognises exceptional achievements in physical, engineering and informatic sciences (including mathematics) by an early career researcher. Awardees are required to have a Scottish connection but can be based anywhere in the world. The prize was founded in 1855 by [Thomas Makdougall Brisbane](/wiki/Thomas_Makdougall_Brisbane "Thomas Makdougall Brisbane"), the long-serving fourth president of the Society.[[18]](#cite_note-18) The medal was renamed in 2022 to reflect [Margaret Moir](/wiki/Margaret,_Lady_Moir "Margaret, Lady Moir")'s contribution to science in Scotland.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Gunning Victoria Jubilee Prize

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Society_of_Edinburgh&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Gunning Victoria Jubilee Prize")]

Main article: [Gunning Victoria Jubilee Prize](/wiki/Gunning_Victoria_Jubilee_Prize "Gunning Victoria Jubilee Prize")

The Gunning Victoria Jubilee Prize Lectureship is a quadrennial award to recognise original work done by scientists resident in or connected with Scotland. The award was founded in 1887 by [Robert Halliday Gunning](/wiki/Robert_Halliday_Gunning "Robert Halliday Gunning"), a Scottish surgeon, entrepreneur and philanthropist who spent much of his life in Brazil.

## Bruce-Preller Lectures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Society_of_Edinburgh&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Bruce-Preller Lectures")]

Main article: [Charles Preller § Bruce-Preller Lectures](/wiki/Charles_Preller#Bruce-Preller_Lectures "Charles Preller")

This biennial lecture given at the Society was begun in 1931 at the bequest of [Charles Preller](/wiki/Charles_Preller "Charles Preller") and named after himself and his late wife, Rachel Steuart Bruce. It is usually (but not invariably) given by a Fellow either of the Royal Society of Edinburgh or the [Royal Society of London](/wiki/Royal_Society_of_London "Royal Society of London").

## Presidents

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Society_of_Edinburgh&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Presidents")]

Presidents of the Royal Society of Edinburgh have included:

1. [Henry Scott](/wiki/Henry_Scott,_3rd_Duke_of_Buccleuch "Henry Scott, 3rd Duke of Buccleuch") (1783–1812)
2. [James Hall](/wiki/Sir_James_Hall,_4th_Baronet "Sir James Hall, 4th Baronet") (1812–1820)
3. [Walter Scott](/wiki/Walter_Scott "Walter Scott") (1820–1832)
4. [Thomas Makdougall Brisbane](/wiki/Thomas_Makdougall_Brisbane "Thomas Makdougall Brisbane") (1832–1860)
5. [George Campbell](/wiki/George_Campbell,_8th_Duke_of_Argyll "George Campbell, 8th Duke of Argyll") (1860–1864)
6. [David Brewster](/wiki/David_Brewster "David Brewster") (1864–1868)
7. [Robert Christison](/wiki/Robert_Christison "Robert Christison") (1869–1873)
8. [William Thomson](/wiki/William_Thomson,_1st_Baron_Kelvin "William Thomson, 1st Baron Kelvin") (later Lord Kelvin) (1873–1878)
9. [Philip Kelland](/wiki/Philip_Kelland "Philip Kelland") (1878–1879)
10. [James Moncreiff](/wiki/James_Moncreiff,_1st_Baron_Moncreiff "James Moncreiff, 1st Baron Moncreiff") (1879–1884)
11. [Thomas Stevenson](/wiki/Thomas_Stevenson "Thomas Stevenson") (1884–1885)
12. William Thomson (later Lord Kelvin) (1886–1890)
13. [Douglas Maclagan](/wiki/Douglas_Maclagan "Douglas Maclagan") (1890–1895)
14. Lord Kelvin (1895–1907)
15. [William Turner](/wiki/William_Turner_(university_principal) "William Turner (university principal)") (1908–1913)
16. [James Geikie](/wiki/James_Geikie "James Geikie") (1913–1915)
17. [John Horne](/wiki/John_Horne "John Horne") (1915–1919)
18. [Frederick Orpen Bower](/wiki/Frederick_Orpen_Bower "Frederick Orpen Bower") (1919–1924)
19. [Alfred Ewing](/wiki/Alfred_Ewing "Alfred Ewing") (1924–1929)
20. [Edward Sharpey Schafer](/wiki/Edward_Sharpey_Schafer "Edward Sharpey Schafer") (1929–1934)
21. [D'Arcy Wentworth Thompson](/wiki/D%27Arcy_Wentworth_Thompson "D'Arcy Wentworth Thompson") (1934–1939)
22. [Edmund Whittaker](/wiki/Edmund_Whittaker "Edmund Whittaker") (1939–1944)
23. [William Wright Smith](/wiki/William_Wright_Smith "William Wright Smith") (1944–1949)
24. [James Kendall](/wiki/James_Kendall_(chemist) "James Kendall (chemist)") (1949–1954)
25. [James Ritchie](/wiki/James_Ritchie_(naturalist) "James Ritchie (naturalist)") (1954–1958)
26. [J. Norman Davidson](/wiki/J._Norman_Davidson "J. Norman Davidson") (1958–1959)
27. [Edmund Hirst](/wiki/Edmund_Hirst "Edmund Hirst") (1959–1964)
28. J. Norman Davidson (1964–1967)
29. [Norman Feather](/wiki/Norman_Feather "Norman Feather") (1967–1970)
30. [Maurice Yonge](/wiki/Maurice_Yonge "Maurice Yonge") (1970–1973)
31. [John Cameron](/wiki/John_Cameron,_Lord_Cameron "John Cameron, Lord Cameron") (1973–1976)
32. [Robert Allan Smith](/wiki/Robert_Allan_Smith "Robert Allan Smith") (1976–1979)
33. [Kenneth Blaxter](/wiki/Kenneth_Blaxter_(animal_nutritionist) "Kenneth Blaxter (animal nutritionist)") (1979–1982)
34. [John Atwell](/wiki/John_Atwell_(engineer) "John Atwell (engineer)") (1982–1985)
35. [Alwyn Williams](/wiki/Alwyn_Williams_(geologist) "Alwyn Williams (geologist)") (1985–1988)
36. [Charles Kemball](/wiki/Charles_Kemball "Charles Kemball") (1988–1991)
37. [Alastair Currie](/wiki/Alastair_Currie "Alastair Currie") (1991–1993)
38. [Thomas L. Johnston](/wiki/Thomas_L._Johnston "Thomas L. Johnston") (1993–1996)
39. [Malcolm Jeeves](/wiki/Malcolm_Jeeves "Malcolm Jeeves") (1996–1999)
40. [William Stewart](/wiki/William_Stewart_(scientist) "William Stewart (scientist)") (1999–2002)
41. [Stewart Sutherland](/wiki/Stewart_Sutherland "Stewart Sutherland") (2002–2005)
42. [Michael Atiyah](/wiki/Michael_Atiyah "Michael Atiyah") (2005–2008)
